name: pipes
context: Chain commands with pipes, redirect stdin/stdout/stderr, and use tee, xargs, and process substitution
info: Pipes and redirection are the UNIX composition model — small commands connected together to build powerful data pipelines. This context covers the pipe operator (|), all forms of I/O redirection (>, >>, <, 2>, &>, <<<), here-documents and here-strings, tee for splitting output, xargs for converting stdin to arguments, process substitution (<() and >()), and named pipes (FIFOs). Agents use pipes constantly: filtering log output, transforming data between formats, searching file contents, counting results, and chaining tools. Understanding pipes is essential for effective shell use. See [[shell/bash]] for command syntax, [[shell/files]] for file-based I/O, and [[shell/processes]] for the process model that underlies pipes.
body:
# Pipes and Redirection

The pipe (`|`) is UNIX's composition operator. It connects the stdout of one command to the stdin of the next, allowing small tools to be chained into powerful pipelines.

## Pipes

```bash
# Basic pipe — feed output of one command into another
ls -la | grep '.js'
cat server.log | grep 'ERROR' | wc -l
ps aux | sort -k 4 -rn | head -10     # Top 10 by memory

# Multi-stage pipeline
find . -name '*.py' -type f |
  xargs grep -l 'import requests' |
  sort |
  uniq

# Pipe to while loop
find . -name '*.log' | while IFS= read -r file; do
  echo "Processing: $file"
  gzip "$file"
done
```

Each pipe segment runs as a separate process. All processes in the pipeline start simultaneously — data flows through as it's produced (streaming, not buffered).

## Output Redirection

```bash
# Redirect stdout to file (overwrite)
command > output.txt

# Redirect stdout to file (append)
command >> output.txt

# Redirect stderr to file
command 2> errors.txt

# Redirect both stdout and stderr to file
command > all.log 2>&1         # Traditional (order matters!)
command &> all.log              # Bash shorthand

# Redirect stdout and stderr to different files
command > output.txt 2> errors.txt

# Discard output
command > /dev/null             # Discard stdout
command 2> /dev/null            # Discard stderr
command &> /dev/null            # Discard everything

# Redirect stderr to stdout (combine for piping)
command 2>&1 | grep 'pattern'
```

### File Descriptor Numbers

| FD | Name | Purpose |
|----|------|---------|
| 0 | stdin | Standard input |
| 1 | stdout | Standard output |
| 2 | stderr | Standard error |
| 3+ | Custom | Opened by scripts for additional I/O |

```bash
# Open file on fd 3 for reading
exec 3< input.txt
read -r line <&3
exec 3<&-                   # Close fd 3

# Open file on fd 4 for writing
exec 4> output.txt
echo "hello" >&4
exec 4>&-                   # Close fd 4
```

## Input Redirection

```bash
# Read file as stdin
command < input.txt
while IFS= read -r line; do
  echo "$line"
done < input.txt

# Here-document (multi-line input)
cat <<EOF
Hello $USER,
Today is $(date).
EOF

# Here-document (no expansion — single-quoted delimiter)
cat <<'EOF'
Literal $USER and $(date) are not expanded.
EOF

# Here-string (single-line input)
grep 'pattern' <<< "$variable"
read -r first rest <<< "hello world"

# Indented here-document (tabs stripped with <<-)
	cat <<-EOF
		Indented content.
		Tabs before delimiter are stripped.
	EOF
```

## tee — Split Output

`tee` copies stdin to both stdout AND one or more files. Essential for logging while also displaying output.

```bash
# Write to file AND display on screen
command | tee output.log

# Append to file
command | tee -a output.log

# Write to multiple files
command | tee file1.log file2.log

# Tee in a pipeline (log intermediate results)
command1 | tee debug.log | command2

# Tee stderr
command 2>&1 | tee all.log

# Write to file that requires sudo
echo 'content' | sudo tee /etc/config.conf > /dev/null
```

## xargs — Build Commands from stdin

`xargs` converts stdin lines into arguments for a command. Essential when piping lists of items to commands that don't read stdin.

```bash
# Basic: pass stdin as arguments
find . -name '*.tmp' | xargs rm

# Handle filenames with spaces/special chars
find . -name '*.tmp' -print0 | xargs -0 rm

# Limit arguments per command invocation
echo "a b c d e f" | xargs -n 2 echo
# Output: a b
#         c d
#         e f

# Parallel execution
find . -name '*.jpg' -print0 | xargs -0 -P 4 -I{} convert {} {}.png

# Use placeholder for argument position
find . -name '*.bak' | xargs -I{} mv {} /backup/

# Confirm before each execution
find . -name '*.tmp' | xargs -p rm

# Practical agent patterns
grep -rl 'old_function' src/ | xargs sed -i 's/old_function/new_function/g'
git diff --name-only | xargs wc -l
cat urls.txt | xargs -P 8 -I{} curl -sO {}
```

## Process Substitution

Process substitution (`<()` and `>()`) creates a temporary file descriptor that acts as a file. Available in Bash and Zsh (not POSIX sh).

```bash
# Compare output of two commands
diff <(ls dir1/) <(ls dir2/)

# Feed command output as a file argument
wc -l <(find . -name '*.py')

# Avoid subshell variable loss in while loops
while IFS= read -r line; do
  count=$((count + 1))
done < <(find . -name '*.txt')
# $count is available here (unlike piping to while)

# Write to multiple destinations
command | tee >(gzip > archive.gz) >(wc -l > count.txt) > full.txt
```

## Named Pipes (FIFOs)

Named pipes are persistent pipe endpoints in the filesystem.

```bash
# Create named pipe
mkfifo /tmp/mypipe

# Writer (blocks until reader connects)
echo "hello" > /tmp/mypipe

# Reader (in another shell)
cat < /tmp/mypipe

# Cleanup
rm /tmp/mypipe
```

## Common Pipeline Patterns

```bash
# Count occurrences
grep -c 'ERROR' logfile.txt
grep 'ERROR' logfile.txt | wc -l        # Equivalent

# Extract and transform
cat data.csv | cut -d',' -f2 | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# Top N results
du -sh */ | sort -rh | head -10

# Filter, transform, and format
ps aux | awk '{print $1, $2, $11}' | sort | column -t

# JSON processing with jq
curl -s 'https://api.example.com/data' | jq '.items[] | .name'

# Watch pipeline output change over time
watch -n 2 'ps aux | grep node | grep -v grep'
```

## Pipeline Error Handling

```bash
# By default, pipe exit code = last command's exit code
false | true
echo $?   # 0 (true succeeded, false failure is hidden)

# set -o pipefail: pipe fails if ANY command fails
set -o pipefail
false | true
echo $?   # 1 (false failed)

# PIPESTATUS array (Bash): exit codes of all pipeline commands
command1 | command2 | command3
echo "${PIPESTATUS[@]}"   # e.g., "0 1 0"
```

sections.title: Pipe operator
sections.body: The `|` operator connects stdout of the left command to stdin of the right command. All commands in the pipeline start simultaneously — data streams through without buffering the entire output. Each pipe segment runs in its own subshell process. By default, the pipeline exit code is the exit code of the last command. Use `set -o pipefail` to make it fail if ANY command fails. Use `${PIPESTATUS[@]}` to inspect individual exit codes.

sections.title: I/O redirection
sections.body: `>` overwrites a file with stdout. `>>` appends. `<` reads a file into stdin. `2>` redirects stderr. `2>&1` merges stderr into stdout. `&>` redirects both (Bash shorthand). `> /dev/null` discards output. File descriptors 0 (stdin), 1 (stdout), 2 (stderr). Custom FDs with `exec 3>file` / `exec 3<file`. Order matters: `command > file 2>&1` works, `command 2>&1 > file` does not (stderr goes to original stdout).

sections.title: xargs — stdin to arguments
sections.body: `xargs` reads items from stdin and passes them as arguments to a command. Essential when piping to commands that don't read stdin (rm, mv, chmod). Use `-0` with `find -print0` for safe handling of filenames with spaces. Use `-I{}` for placement control. Use `-n N` to limit args per invocation. Use `-P N` for parallel execution. Use `-p` for interactive confirmation. Common: `find . -name '*.tmp' -print0 | xargs -0 rm`, `git diff --name-only | xargs wc -l`.

sections.title: Process substitution
sections.body: `<(command)` creates a file descriptor containing command's output — usable wherever a filename is expected. `>(command)` creates a file descriptor that writes to command's stdin. Bash/Zsh only (not POSIX). Key use: `diff <(cmd1) <(cmd2)` to compare outputs. Also avoids subshell variable loss: `while read line; do ...; done < <(find ...)` preserves variables (unlike `find | while`). Uses /dev/fd/ internally.

sections.title: Here-documents and here-strings
sections.body: Here-document (`<<DELIM`): multi-line input block. Variables and command substitutions are expanded. Quote the delimiter (`<<'DELIM'`) to prevent all expansion. Indent with `<<-DELIM` (strips leading tabs only). Here-string (`<<<`): single-line input from a variable or literal — `grep pattern <<< "$text"`. Heredocs are essential for writing multi-line content to files, passing structured input to commands, and creating config files from scripts.

related.note: Pipe and redirection are Bash syntax fundamentals
